headline: Sam can do the work, but week three is dominated by unclear “ready to ship” expectations and approval/quality bars.
summary_bullets: Sam second-guesses his approach and loses time when design and API details don’t match, so the “what to do” is there but the “how it should land” isn’t.
The biggest friction point is approval and quality-bar ownership: “I am not always sure who should approve what before I push.”
He waits to ask because he wants to solve first, and he moves faster only when feedback is “specific and fast,” so the onboarding handoffs need to be made earlier.
understanding_paragraph: Sam is landing on the basics and can execute tasks, but he’s still missing the concrete expectations that make shipping feel safe. The session clarified that his hesitation isn’t about effort—it’s about approval sequencing and differences in quality bars between quick fixes and core flows. He also has a clear coping pattern: he delays asking until he’s stuck, which turns small uncertainties into lost time.

axes.meaning: Weak signal of stress from uncertainty before shipping; worth watching, not a burnout verdict.
No strong engagement signal; he’s willing to pair and ask, but momentum depends on clearer support.
Defining signal: role expectations for approvals and quality bars are unclear, directly slowing shipping.
A real pattern: he improves quickly with fast, specific feedback, but currently lacks the guidance to progress independently.

brutal_truth_employee: Sam is capable of doing the tasks, but he’s stuck on “second-guess whether my approach matches team expectations.” He also waits too long to ask—“I usually wait too long before asking because I want to solve things myself first”—and he only speeds up when feedback is “specific and fast.”
brutal_truth_manager: Sam gave a clear, actionable signal about the approval/quality-bar gap: “I am not always sure who should approve what before I push.” Next time, go one level deeper on the exact handoff points (who approves, at what stage, with what checklist) instead of letting the conversation stay at “pairing” as the only fix.

next_actions.action: Schedule a 30-minute pairing on Sam’s next ticket to walk through the exact approval bar (who signs off, when, and what evidence counts).
Create a one-page “quality bar” cheat sheet for Sam: quick fix vs core flow, with concrete examples and a short checklist for each.
Confirm the two-ticket definition of “good looks like” and ask Sam to name the first handoff he would ask about earlier next time.

watch_for: Within two weeks: if Sam asks earlier (before pushing) and can name the approver for his current ticket, the clarity fix is working.
Within two weeks: if Sam still “wait[s] too long before asking,” then the handoff/approval map is still missing or too hard to use.
